figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of Amortized Physics-Informed Calibration (APIC): The framework employs a dual-branch latentvariable Neural Process architecture to disentangle observed system behavior into parametric physics and a structural discrepancy. The Physics Branch (top) infers instance-specific parameters θ to drive a differentiable simulator F , while the Discrepancy Branch (bottom) learns a shared, state-dependent correction model δ. By combining these components, APIC achieves calibration and uncertainty quantification for unseen realizations of imperfect physical systems.

Table 1: Quantitative comparison for damped spring system. † methods are corrected per realization and not amortized as our method. Best results are shown in bold, and the second-best results are underlined.

Table 2: Quantitative results for Lotka-Volterra ODE System. † methods are corrected per realization and not amortized as our method. Best results are shown in bold, and the second-best results are underlined.

Figure 3: Qualitative correction pipeline for the Lotka-Volterra ODE System. From left to right: sparse context observations; prediction from the misspecified nominal simulator; learned discrepancy; corrected prediction obtained by combining simulator and discrepancy; and the ground-truth solution. The blue plot refers to the prey and the red plot is the predator.

Figure 4: Qualitative correction pipeline for the advection–diffusion system. From left to right: sparse context observations; prediction from the misspecified nominal simulator; learned discrepancy field; corrected prediction obtained by combining simulator and discrepancy; and the ground-truth solution. APIC identifies and compensates for the structured model bias, recovering the true spatio-temporal dynamics.

Figure 5: Qualitative reconstruction results for the Lotka–Volterra system. Ground-truth predator and prey populations are shown alongside APIC’s posterior predictive mean and ±2 standard deviations. The model accurately captures the oscillatory dynamics, and the uncertainty bands reliably cover the true trajectories under sparse observations.

Figure 6: Qualitative discrepancy prediction for the Lotka–Volterra system. The learned discrepancy for predator and prey populations is shown alongside the ground-truth correction and predictive uncertainty (±2 standard deviations).

Figure 8: Discrepancy analysis for the advection–diffusion system. The plot shows the predicted discrepancy, the groundtruth discrepancy, the absolute error, and the predictive uncertainty. Regions with higher prediction error correspond to increased uncertainty, indicating calibrated confidence in the learned correction.

Figure 9: Effect of context set size on predictive performance for the damped spring system. Reconstruction, discrepancy, and parameter MAE are shown as the number of context points increases from 10 to 100. Errors decrease sharply in the low-data regime and stabilize beyond approximately 30 context points.

Figure 10: Effect of observation noise on predictive performance for the damped spring system. Reconstruction, discrepancy, and parameter MAE are reported as noise magnitude increases. Errors grow gradually with noise, indicating stable and robust inference under moderate perturbations.

Figure 11: Ablation analysis on the influence of λθ (green) and λδ (yellow) on (a) reconstruction MAE, (b) Discrepancy MAE and (c) Physics parameter θ MAE. With increasing values of λθ and λδ , the MAE values decrease.
